sn46.56:1.3: Prince Abhaya (“fearless”) raised Jīvaka after discovering him abandoned Kd 8:1.4.5. In this sutta he asks the Buddha about Pūraṇa Kassapa, whereas in MN 58 he appears as an acolyte of Mahāvīra. Apart from these details he is unknown in the early texts. Commentaries say he was the son of King Bimbisāra by one of his queens, Padumavatī of Ujjenī, making him half-brother to Ajātasattu. They also say that following Bimbisāra’s death, Abhaya went forth and spoke the verse at Thag 1.26. | Not to be confused with Abhaya the Licchavī of AN 3.74 and AN 4.196; nor with the author of Thag 1.98; nor is Thig 2.8 addressed to his mother, but that of the nun Abhayā (Thig 2.9).
sn46.56:1.5: In DN 2, Pūraṇa Kassapa posits the view that there are no results of deeds. There, Makkhali argues that there is no cause for the purification of beings, but the same view is attributed to Pūraṇa as well (SN 22.60:2.1). The exact view stated here does not appear elsewhere in the Pali canon, but does not contradict Pūraṇa’s other views. If action has no consequence, then there is nothing we can do to gain wisdom. This view also explains why his own students did not respect his understanding (MN 77:6.22). Nonetheless, he claimed to be omniscient (AN 9.38:2.1). His doctrine of “six classes” (AN 6.57) is shared with Makkhali (DN 2:20.6). This suggests that, like Makkhali, he believed in a predestined course of rebirths, culminating in the highest rebirth as an omniscient one, per the “rolling out of a ball of string” (DN 2:20.10).
